title: LINQ expressions should be simplified
description: LINQ expressions should be simplifiedAnalyze your codeadaptability-focusedmaintainabilityCode SmellclumsyWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoIn the interests of readability, code that can be simplified should be simplified. To that end, there are several waysIEnumerablelanguage integrated queries (LINQ) can be simplified. This not only improves readabilty but can also lead to improved performance.Available In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CI©2025SonarSource Sàrl.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
// Noncompliant: Unnecessarily complex LINQ expression
var result = list.Where(x => x != null).Count(); // Noncompliant

// Noncompliant: Can be simplified
var result = list.Select(x => x).ToList(); // Noncompliant

// Noncompliant: Redundant Any()
var result = list.Where(x => x > 0).Any() == false; // Noncompliant
compliant:
// Compliant: Simplified LINQ expression
var result = list.Count(x => x != null); // Compliant

// Compliant: Direct ToList()
var result = list.ToList(); // Compliant

// Compliant: Using negation
var result = !list.Where(x => x > 0).Any(); // Compliant

logic.reasoning: Detects LINQ expressions that can be simplified: Where().Count() should be Count(predicate), Select(x => x) is redundant, Where().Any() == false should use negation. Uses custom function for proper method-level test exclusion.
